edges.description: Insufficient thyroid hormone production from dysgenetic thyroid tissue leads to thyroid hormone deficiency during critical periods of brain development.
edges.target: Neurodevelopmental Impairment from Thyroid Hormone Deficiency

edges.description: Impaired thyroid hormone synthesis leads to the same downstream thyroid hormone deficiency as dysgenesis, though the thyroid gland is present and often goitrous.
edges.target: Neurodevelopmental Impairment from Thyroid Hormone Deficiency

edges.description: Dyshormonogenesis can produce goitrous congenital hypothyroidism with thyroid enlargement.
Inadequate TSH drive results in insufficient thyroid hormone production, causing the same downstream neurodevelopmental risk.
edges.target: Neurodevelopmental Impairment from Thyroid Hormone Deficiency

edges.description: Central congenital hypothyroidism can present with prolonged neonatal jaundice.
intermediate_mechanisms: Low TSH drive causes central congenital hypothyroidism.

edges.description: Severe central congenital hypothyroidism can manifest with neonatal hypotonia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Low TSH drive causes severe central congenital hypothyroidism.

edges.description: Severe central congenital hypothyroidism can cause macroglossia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Low TSH drive causes severe central congenital hypothyroidism.

edges.description: Severe central congenital hypothyroidism can manifest with umbilical hernia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Low TSH drive causes severe central congenital hypothyroidism.

edges.description: Even transient thyroid hormone deficiency during critical neonatal brain development may cause neurodevelopmental harm if untreated.
edges.target: Neurodevelopmental Impairment from Thyroid Hormone Deficiency

edges.description: Untreated thyroid hormone deficiency during early brain development can cause intellectual disability.
edges.source: Neurodevelopmental Impairment from Thyroid Hormone Deficiency

edges.target: Neurodevelopmental Impairment from Thyroid Hormone Deficiency

nodes.description: Thyroid hormone deficiency present at birth, detectable by newborn screening.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: The incidence of CH partly depends on the screening strategy; based on data from a number of screening programs, the incidence of primary CH lies between 1 in 3000 and 1 in 2000; the highest reported incidence of central CH is ∼1 in 16,000

nodes.description: Inborn errors of thyroid hormone synthesis due to mutations in genes encoding enzymes and transporters required for iodide organification and thyroid hormone biosynthesis (TPO, TG, DUOX2, SLC5A5, SLC26A4), typically resulting in goitrous hypothyroidism.

evidence.reference_title: The genetic characteristics of congenital hypothyroidism in China by comprehensive screening of 21 candidate genes.
evidence.snippet: Our results showed that 57 patients (51.82%) carried biallelic mutations (containing compound heterozygous mutations and homozygous mutations) in six genes (DUOX2, DUOXA2, DUOXA1, TG, TPO and TSHR) involved in thyroid hormone synthesis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical characteristics and genotype-phenotype analysis of congenital hypothyroidism diagnosed by newborn screening in China.
evidence.snippet: DUOX2 gene had the highest variant rate, followed by TG, TPO and TSHR gene.

nodes.description: Thyroid enlargement occurs in dyshormonogenesis subtypes where the gland is present but hormone synthesis is impaired, and may develop nodules requiring monitoring.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical characteristics and genotype-phenotype analysis of congenital hypothyroidism diagnosed by newborn screening in China.
evidence.snippet: The "DUOX2 biallelic variants" group was associated with "Goiter"

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: Children and adolescents with primary CH due to dyshomonogenesis may develop goiter and nodules; in these cases, serum TSH should be carefully targeted in the lower part of normal range and periodical ultrasound investigation is recommended to monitor thyroid volume

nodes.description: Decreased muscle tone is commonly observed in infants with congenital hypothyroidism.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: Isolated central CH due to biallelic TSHβ gene mutations is associated with severe hypothyroidism and characterized by the typical manifestations of CH (hypotonia, jaundice, umbilical hernia, macroglossia, etc.).

nodes.description: Deficient hypothalamic TRH signaling or pituitary thyrotroph dysfunction leads to inadequate TSH secretion, producing low free T4 with inappropriately low or normal TSH.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: If the serum fT4 is low, and TSH is low, normal or slightly elevated, the diagnosis central CH should be considered

nodes.description: If untreated, congenital hypothyroidism leads to intellectual disability due to the critical role of thyroid hormone in brain development.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: Congenital hypothyroidism (CH) is the one of the most common preventable cause of mental retardation.

nodes.description: Levothyroxine is the standard treatment for congenital hypothyroidism. Early initiation within 2 weeks of birth at doses of 10-15 mcg/kg/day is critical to prevent irreversible neurodevelopmental damage.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: LT4 treatment should be started as soon as possible, not later than 2 weeks after birth or immediately after confirmatory (serum) thyroid function testing

evidence.reference_title: Congenital hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: Treatment should be started promptly and infant should be rendered euthyroid as early as possible, as there is an inverse relationship between intelligence quotient (IQ) and the age at diagnosis.

nodes.description: Enlarged tongue is a classic clinical feature of untreated congenital hypothyroidism.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: Isolated central CH due to biallelic TSHβ gene mutations is associated with severe hypothyroidism and characterized by the typical manifestations of CH (hypotonia, jaundice, umbilical hernia, macroglossia, etc.).

nodes.description: Thyroid hormone is indispensable for brain development during fetal life and the first 2-3 years. Deficiency during this critical period causes irreversible neurodevelopmental damage if untreated, historically known as cretinism.
nodes.id: Neurodevelopmental Impairment from Thyroid Hormone Deficiency

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: Congenital hypothyroidism is common and can cause severe neurodevelopmental morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: delayed diagnosis leads to the most severe outcome of CH, mental retardation, emphasizing the importance of neonatal screening.

nodes.description: Universal newborn screening programs detect congenital hypothyroidism through TSH and/or T4 measurement from heel-prick dried blood spots, enabling early treatment before clinical symptoms develop.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: Early detection and treatment of CH through neonatal screening prevent irreversible neurodevelopmental delay and optimize its developmental outcome

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: Neonatal screening for CH has almost eliminated the profound negative effects of TH deficiency on growth and neurodevelopment (cretinism) in those countries where it has been established.

nodes.description: Prolonged jaundice beyond the first two weeks of life is a common early clinical sign of congenital hypothyroidism in neonates.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: Isolated central CH due to biallelic TSHβ gene mutations is associated with severe hypothyroidism and characterized by the typical manifestations of CH (hypotonia, jaundice, umbilical hernia, macroglossia, etc.).

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: we recommend endocrine testing in all neonates with a familial history of central CH, or signs or symptoms of congenital hypopituitarism, for example, micropenis with undescended testes, hypoglycemia, prolonged jaundice, or unexplained failure to thrive.

nodes.description: The most common cause of permanent primary congenital hypothyroidism is abnormal thyroid gland development, including thyroid agenesis, ectopy, or hypoplasia, resulting in insufficient thyroid hormone production from birth. Thyroid dysgenesis accounts for 65-85% of primary CH cases.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular Genetics of Primary Congenital Hypothyroidism: Established and Emerging Contributors to Thyroid Dysgenesis.
evidence.snippet: The primary form of CH is attributable to thyroid dysgenesis (agenesis, hypoplasia, or ectopy) in 65-85% of cases, with the remaining cases being attributed to dyshormogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: In the majority of patients, CH is caused by an abnormal development of the thyroid gland (thyroid dysgenesis) that is a sporadic disorder and accounts for 85% of cases and the remaining 15% of cases are caused by dyshormonogenesis.

nodes.description: Transient congenital hypothyroidism results from reversible disruption of thyroid function, most commonly due to transplacental passage of maternal TSH receptor-blocking antibodies, exposure to excess iodine, or hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis immaturity in premature infants.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital hypothyroidism.
evidence.snippet: Transient CH most commonly occurs in preterm infants born in areas of endemic iodine deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: the prevalence of transient CH has steadily increased. In a number of studies, factors have been identified that increase the likelihood of transient disease, such as sex (more often in boys)

nodes.description: Umbilical hernia is a classic clinical sign of congenital hypothyroidism in newborns.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital Hypothyroidism: A 2020-2021 Consensus Guidelines Update.
evidence.snippet: Isolated central CH due to biallelic TSHβ gene mutations is associated with severe hypothyroidism and characterized by the typical manifestations of CH (hypotonia, jaundice, umbilical hernia, macroglossia, etc.).
